section.title: A Call to Return
section.sectionRationale: After the affliction that ends chapter 5, the people frame a call to return. But their repentance proves shallow, and God answers with the heart of Hosea's whole message: He desires steadfast love and the knowledge of God, not empty ritual. The chapter then re-exposes a covenant broken like Adam's.
section.thesis: Israel voices a hopeful call to return to the LORD who heals, yet God pierces their fleeting loyalty and declares what He truly wants: chesed, steadfast love, and the knowledge of God, more than sacrifice.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the people's call to return and its bright hope of healing; God's grief over a loyalty that vanishes like mist; and the great charge, mercy not sacrifice, against a covenant broken like Adam's.

verses.text: Come, let us {{shuv|return}} to the LORD. For He has torn us to pieces, but He will heal us; He has wounded us, but He will bind up our wounds.
After two days He will revive us; on the third day He will raise us up, that we may live in His presence.
So let us know, let us press on to know the LORD. As surely as the sun rises, He will appear; He will come to us like the rain, like the spring showers that water the earth.
What shall I do with you, O Ephraim? What shall I do with you, O Judah? For your {{chesed|loyalty}} is like a morning mist, like the early dew that vanishes.
Therefore I have hewn them by the prophets; I have slain them by the words of My mouth, and My judgments go forth like lightning.

units.label: Mercy, not sacrifice
units.note: the heart of what God desires

verses.text: For I desire {{chesed|mercy}}, not sacrifice, and the {{daat|knowledge}} of God rather than burnt offerings.
But they, like Adam, have transgressed the covenant; there they were unfaithful to Me.
Gilead is a city of evildoers, tracked with footprints of blood.
Like raiders who lie in ambush, so does a band of priests; they murder on the way to Shechem; surely they have committed atrocities.
In the house of Israel I have seen a horrible thing: Ephraim practices prostitution there, and Israel is defiled.
Also for you, O Judah, a harvest is appointed, when I restore My people from captivity.

shuv.gloss: To turn, to return, to repent. It is the great prophetic word for repentance, a turning back to the covenant LORD. Israel speaks the right word here, come let us return, but as the chapter shows, the turning must be of the heart and not only the lips.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast love, loyal covenant devotion, mercy. It is the loyal kindness partners owe within a covenant, and the love God shows and delights to receive. In verse 4 Israel's chesed is fleeting like dew; in verse 6 God declares He desires this very chesed more than sacrifice.

daat.gloss: Knowledge, from the root yada, to know intimately. In Hosea it is a living, relational knowledge of God, not mere information. God pairs it with chesed as what He truly desires, so that true religion is loving, loyal, personal knowing of the LORD rather than outward ritual.

basic.context: Stung by God's discipline, the people compose a song of return: the LORD who tore us will heal us, and on the third day raise us up. It sounds right, and the promise of God coming like the spring rains is beautiful. But God sees that their loyalty is as short-lived as morning mist, gone by midday. Then comes the verse at the heart of the whole book: God desires steadfast love and the knowledge of Himself, not just sacrifices. Israel, like Adam, has broken the covenant, and even the priests are stained with violence.
basic.hermeneutics: The opening call to return can be read as genuine or as a too-easy formula; God's reply in verse 4 shows He weighs the heart behind the words. Verse 6 is not a rejection of sacrifice as such, but a prophetic priority: ritual without love and knowledge of God is worthless. Jesus twice quotes this verse to interpret His own ministry.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel speaks the great word shuv, return or repent, but their chesed, steadfast love, evaporates like dew. God's answer names what He wants: chesed and the daat, knowledge, of God, more than burnt offerings. True religion is loyal love and personal knowledge of the LORD, not mere ritual.
basic.typology: The hope of being raised up on the third day to live in His presence foreshadows the resurrection, supremely Christ raised on the third day, and with Him His people. The priority of mercy over sacrifice is fulfilled in Christ, who both shows perfect mercy and offers the one sacrifice that ritual only pictured.
basic.christ: Jesus takes up verse 6 as His own banner, twice telling the Pharisees, Go and learn what this means: I desire mercy, not sacrifice. He is the mercy of God in person, eating with sinners, and the one who perfectly knows the Father and makes Him known. The third day hope of resurrection is fulfilled when God raises Jesus, and in Him raises His people to live in His presence.
basic.god: God is the Healer who wounds only to restore, and He longs to come to His people like life-giving rain. He is not fooled by shallow repentance; He desires the heart, not the performance. What He wants most is loyal love and to be truly known, which tells us the living God seeks relationship, not mere religious transactions.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 9:13 and Matthew 12:7 record Jesus quoting I desire mercy, not sacrifice. 1 Samuel 15:22 says to obey is better than sacrifice. 1 Corinthians 15:4 proclaims Christ raised on the third day.
basic.oneStory: Chapter 6 holds out the hope of a God who heals and raises on the third day, and names what He truly desires, love and knowledge of Himself. Both are fulfilled in Christ, who is the mercy of God, who knows the Father perfectly, and who is raised on the third day to bring His people into God's presence.

context.literary: The chapter pivots from the affliction of chapter 5 to a liturgy of return, then to God's searching reply. It pairs a lovely confession, He will heal us, with a sober verdict, your loyalty is like mist, and reaches its peak in the terse charge of verse 6.
context.historical: The call to return may echo the shallow national repentance that followed military defeat. Gilead and Shechem, named for bloodshed, and the band of priests who murder, reflect the lawlessness of Israel's final decades. The reference to Adam evokes either the first man's covenant breaking or a place called Adam.
context.canonical: Verse 6, mercy over sacrifice, becomes a load-bearing text in the prophets and in Jesus' teaching. The third day resurrection hope threads from here through Jonah and the Psalms to its fulfillment in the risen Christ, whom Paul says was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the difference between a repentance of words and a returning of the heart, and to declare God's abiding priority, that He desires steadfast love and the knowledge of Himself above outward sacrifice.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The people's call to return is quoted, then evaluated; the enduring prescription is God's own word in verse 6, to pursue mercy and the knowledge of God, and the ongoing summons to genuine return.

debates.question: Is the call to return in verses 1 to 3 sincere repentance or shallow presumption?

views.label: Genuine words, real hope
views.case: The confession is theologically true, God does heal and raise, and it models what right repentance should say; the fault lies in Israel not living it out.

views.label: Shallow presumption
views.case: God's reply in verse 4 exposes their loyalty as fleeting mist, suggesting the words are a too-easy formula that presumes on grace without heart change.

debates.bearing: Either way the lesson is the same: the right words of repentance must be matched by enduring love and knowledge of God, or they vanish like dew, and only in Christ is such steadfast return made possible.

study.originalLanguages: The people rightly cry shuv, return, the prophetic word for repentance, and their confession is full of hope: God tears but heals, and on the third day will raise them to live in His presence. Yet God's reply cuts through: their chesed, steadfast covenant love, is like morning mist that vanishes. Then comes the heart of Hosea, verse 6: God desires chesed and the daat, knowledge, of God, rather than burnt offerings. These two words, loyal love and personal knowing, define true religion over against hollow ritual, and it is these that Israel, like Adam, has broken.

typology.type: On the third day He will raise us up
typology.fulfillment: Christ raised on the third day, and His people raised with Him to live in God's presence, 1 Corinthians 15:4.
Christ who is the mercy of God in person and the one true sacrifice that all offerings foreshadowed, Matthew 9:13.

typology.type: God coming like the spring rains
typology.fulfillment: Christ who pours out the Spirit like rain on dry ground and gives living water, John 7:38.

study.christ: Verse 6 is so central that Jesus quotes it twice as the key to His mission, telling the self-righteous to go and learn its meaning: I desire mercy, not sacrifice. Christ is that mercy made flesh, welcoming sinners and tax collectors, and He is the one who perfectly knows the Father, the very knowledge Israel lacked. The third day hope, that God will raise us up to live in His presence, is answered when the Father raises Jesus on the third day, the firstfruits of all who will be raised with Him. Where Israel's love was mist, Christ's covenant love never fails, and He becomes for His people the steadfast return they could not make.
study.god: The LORD is a healer who wounds only to restore, and who longs to come to His people like the rain that revives dry land. He is not deceived by fine words; He weighs the heart and grieves over love that evaporates. Most strikingly, He tells us plainly what He desires: not the machinery of religion but loyal love and the knowledge of Himself. This is a God who wants to be known and loved, not merely served, and who prizes mercy in His people because mercy is so deeply His own character.

crossReferences.note: Jesus quotes I desire mercy, not sacrifice to defend eating with sinners.
Jesus cites the same verse to rebuke those who condemn the guiltless while missing mercy.
To obey is better than sacrifice, the same prophetic priority of heart over ritual.
Christ was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures, fulfilling the third day hope.

study.oneStory: Chapter 6 lifts up a God who heals, who raises on the third day, and who desires love and knowledge of Himself above ritual. Every strand is answered in Christ, the mercy of God in person, who knows the Father perfectly and is raised on the third day to bring His people into the presence of God.

quiz.q: What does God say He truly desires in verse 6?

options.text: Steadfast love and the knowledge of God, more than sacrifice and burnt offerings.
options.note: Verse 6 names chesed and the knowledge of God as God's true desire.

options.text: More frequent and costlier sacrifices.
options.note: God is not asking for more ritual; He desires love and knowledge of Himself.

options.text: Longer prayers at the high places.
options.note: The high places were part of the problem; God wants heart, not more religious activity.

options.text: Nothing at all from His people.
options.note: God desires much, but relational love and knowledge, not empty ritual.

quiz.q: How does God describe Israel's loyalty in verse 4?

options.text: Like a morning mist and early dew that quickly vanishes.
options.note: Their chesed evaporates rather than enduring, verse 4.

options.text: Like a deep and steady river.
options.note: The opposite is true; their loyalty does not last.

options.text: Like a strong fortress.
options.note: There is nothing enduring about their loyalty here.

options.text: Perfect and unbroken.
options.note: God grieves precisely because it is fleeting, not perfect.

quiz.q: How does Jesus use verse 6 in the Gospels?

options.text: He quotes I desire mercy, not sacrifice to defend welcoming sinners and to rebuke the merciless.
options.note: Matthew 9:13 and 12:7 record both uses.

options.text: He never refers to this verse.
options.note: In fact He quotes it twice as central to His mission.

options.text: He uses it to abolish all worship.
options.note: He affirms mercy and true worship, not the end of worship.

options.text: He uses it to command more sacrifices.
options.note: He uses it to prioritize mercy over mere ritual.

quiz.q: How does the third day hope of verse 2 point to Christ?

options.text: It foreshadows the resurrection, fulfilled when God raises Jesus on the third day to bring His people into His presence.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 15:4 ties resurrection to the third day.

options.text: It sets an exact calendar for the end of the world.
options.note: It anticipates resurrection life, not a datebook of the end.

options.text: It refers only to a two day military campaign.
options.note: The language reaches beyond politics to revival and resurrection life.

options.text: It has no bearing on Christ.
options.note: The New Testament ties the third day directly to Christ's resurrection.

quiz.q: What is the danger the chapter warns against in repentance?

options.text: Saying the right words of return while the heart's love vanishes like mist.
options.note: Israel speaks of returning, but their loyalty does not endure, verses 1 to 4.

options.text: Repenting too thoroughly and too often.
options.note: The problem is shallow repentance, not excess.

options.text: Using Scripture in prayer.
options.note: The words themselves are fine; the missing heart is the danger.

options.text: Seeking healing from God at all.
options.note: God is glad to heal; He wants a true return, not none.

questions: The people said the right words about returning to God, yet their love faded like mist. Where might your words of repentance be outrunning your heart?
God desires mercy and the knowledge of Him more than religious performance. Which are you more tempted to offer God, activity or genuine relationship?
Jesus told the self-righteous to go and learn what I desire mercy, not sacrifice means. Where do you need to grow in showing mercy rather than keeping score?
God promised to come like the spring rains. In what dry area of your life are you longing for Him to come and revive you?
The third day hope is fulfilled in the risen Christ. How does His resurrection give you confidence that God can heal and raise what feels dead in you?

translationNotes.note: This verse is cited by Jesus in Matthew 9:13 and Matthew 12:7. The Hebrew chesed can be rendered mercy, steadfast love, or loyal devotion; the point is loving loyalty over mere ritual.
Like Adam may also be rendered as at Adam, taking Adam as a place name; either reading pictures a decisive breaking of the covenant.
When I restore My people from captivity may also be rendered when I restore the fortunes of My people, a note of hope amid the judgment.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hosea 6:1-11, with poetic lines joined into running verses. The em dash in the source at verse 3 has been rendered as a comma per house style.
